findings.description: The review claims `build_county_outcome_panel.py` filters QCEW records with `chunk["qtr"] == "A"` (annual average). That means 2020 values blend pre-lockdown Q1 with the Q2 collapse and later recovery, so any 2020 log-change window is structurally contaminated by pandemic timing rather than local housing-capacity dynamics. The reviewer argues this is a self-inflicted artifact because the BLS single-file data already contain quarterly rows that could isolate cleaner comparisons.
findings.fix: Extract quarterly QCEW data instead of annual averages, preferably using a consistent quarter such as Q1-to-Q1 (or another explicitly justified quarter) for 2020/2021 comparisons.
findings.title: QCEW annual-average extraction hardcodes COVID contamination into 2020 outcome windows

findings.description: The review flags `panel["net_us_migration_persons_ty2021_2022_proxy"] = panel["net_us_migration_persons_2022_23"]` in `build_county_outcome_panel.py` as a severe semantic failure. The concern is that 2022-2023 migration occurred under a materially different rate, housing, and return-to-office environment, so equating it to 2021-2022 silently misstates the timing of the migration shock.
findings.fix: Remove the proxy column or replace it with the actual 2021-2022 IRS migration file. If no true file exists, keep the gap explicit rather than encoding a mismatched year as if it were observed.
findings.title: IRS 2022-2023 migration is silently used as a proxy for 2021-2022 migration

findings.description: The review states that `analyze_capacity_falsification.py` uses `str(row["Date"]).startswith(tuple(keep_years))` to retain 2018/2019/2020 rows. This is brittle because varying date formats such as `MM/YYYY`, `YYYYMM`, or parsed timestamps can silently over-select or miss records. The reviewer explicitly calls this a parsing risk that should be audited.
findings.fix: Parse the `Date` field to a real datetime or year integer and filter with an explicit year test such as `pd.to_datetime(df["Date"]).dt.year.isin([...])`.
findings.title: BPS year filtering relies on fragile string-prefix date matching

findings.description: The review argues that the memo's 'diffuse threshold' result may simply reflect discretizing a continuous relationship. It says `analyze_capacity_falsification.py` searches threshold cutoffs but does not test a continuous or log-linear interaction like `surge_load * capacity_constraint` against the threshold specification, so it cannot tell whether the threshold is an artifact of arbitrary binning.
findings.fix: Add continuous and/or log-linear benchmark models and compare fit or out-of-sample performance against the threshold model using metrics such as BIC, R², or holdout error.
findings.title: Threshold search is not benchmarked against a continuous capacity model

findings.description: The review notes that the pipeline isolates private-sector totals with `own_code == "0"` and `industry_code == "10"`, then interprets wage changes across pandemic windows. It argues this is vulnerable to composition bias because layoffs hit lower-wage service workers disproportionately in 2020, mechanically raising average weekly wages among remaining workers. The reviewer says the falsification does not control for initial service-sector share or similar composition measures.
findings.fix: Control for pre-period sector composition, such as baseline service-sector employment share, or rerun wage analyses on sector-adjusted outcomes to separate composition effects from true wage pressure.
findings.title: Pandemic-period wage analyses do not control for sector-composition bias

findings.description: The review says the scripts use 2018-2020 permits as the pre-surge capacity measure, but units permitted in 2020 generally require 12-24 months to complete. That makes 2020 permits a poor proxy for housing stock available when the 2021 immigration surge began. The reviewer recommends defining capacity entering the surge with a lagged 2017-2019 permit window instead.
findings.fix: Shift the permit baseline back one year so pre-surge capacity is built from 2017-2019 permits, or otherwise model permit-to-completion lags explicitly.
findings.title: Pre-surge housing-capacity baseline includes 2020 permits that likely were not available for the 2021 surge

findings.description: The review criticizes the current QCEW ingestion approach in `build_county_outcome_panel.py` for using `pd.read_csv(..., chunksize=500_000)` on large ZIP/CSV inputs. It argues this creates avoidable runtime and memory pressure, especially since the project already uses DuckDB elsewhere and could push filtering into `read_csv_auto(...)` instead of Python-level chunk iteration.
findings.fix: Replace chunked Pandas extraction with a DuckDB query that reads the source files directly and filters rows in SQL before materializing a dataframe.
findings.title: Chunked Pandas QCEW extraction is described as unnecessarily slow and memory-heavy
